nodes.title: Watershed Hydrology
nodes.description: See Schedule of Courses for specific titles. Prerequisite: Senior standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Senior standing.

nodes.title: General Chemistry 2
nodes.description: Second semester of a two-semester sequence. Topics include solutions, kinetics, equilibrium, acid-base chemistry, aqueous ionic equilibria, thermodynamics, electrochemistry, and nuclear chemistry. Credit not awarded for both CHEM 1450 and CHEM 1455. Prerequisite: CHEM 1400 or CHEM 1405.
nodes.other_requirements: $60 fee for lab supplies; Degree students only;

nodes.title: Intro to Envrnmtl Studies
nodes.description: Survey of the interdisciplinary field of environmental studies. Examines ecological, cultural, historical, and political influences on the role of humans in local and global ecosystems and the grand challenges humanity faces. Required for Environmental Studies majors and minors.

nodes.title: SL:Envirnmnt Consltng Capstone
nodes.description: See Schedule of Courses for specific titles.
nodes.other_requirements: Must have senior standing to register.

nodes.title: General Chemistry 1
nodes.description: First semester of a two-semester sequence. Topics include matter, stoichiometry, gas laws, thermochemistry, quantum theory, atomic structure, electronic configurations, bonding, and intermolecular forces. Credit not awarded for both CHEM 1400 and CHEM 1100, CHEM 1102, or CHEM 1405.

nodes.title: Climate Change II
nodes.description: Advanced exploration of how and when climate changed over time; impact on people and ecosystems; how humans have altered Earth's climate historically; how climate will change in the future; what this implies for people and planet. Learn to communicate about climate change and take action. Credit not awarded for both ENSC 1490 and ENSC 2490. Prerequisites: One class in physical or natural sciences or engineering.
nodes.other_requirements: Must register for ENSC discussion labs, D01-D03

nodes.title: Orientation to Env Sciences
nodes.description: Introduces new majors to the discipline, major, and profession through advising exercises, guest lectures, panel discussions and skills practice. Provides a foundation of common disciplinary frameworks and methodologies to support students in major coursework. Prerequisites: First-Year Rubenstein School of Environment and Natural Resources Environmental Science major.
nodes.other_requirements: First-Year Rubenstein School of Environment and Natural Resources Environmental Science major. 1st yr ENSC Majors in CALS/RSENR only

nodes.title: Internship
nodes.description: On-site supervised work experience combined with a structured academic learning plan directed by a faculty member or a faculty-staff team in which a faculty member is the instructor of record, for which academic credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion. Maximum of six hours. Three can be applied to elected concentration with Director permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Director permission. Instructor permission required.

nodes.title: Independent Study
nodes.description: Tailored to the interests of a specific student, occurs outside the traditional classroom/laboratory setting under faculty supervision, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion. Up to six hours. Three can be applied to elected concentration with Director permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Director permission for up to six hours to be applied to elected concentration. Instructor permission required and completion of an Experiential Learning Plan required.

nodes.title: Ecology and Evolution
nodes.description: Ecosystem and community structure, population growth, species interactions and niche dynamics, population and chromosomal genetics, speciation in fossil records, ecology of animal behavior, applied ecology. Prerequisites: BCOR 1400, BIOL 1400, or BIOL 1000; BCOR 1450, BIOL 1450, BIOL 1005, or BCOR 1425; MATH 1212 or MATH 1234.
nodes.other_requirements: PACE students with permission and override.

nodes.title: Calculus II
nodes.description: Vectors and vector operations. Techniques and applications of integration. Polar coordinates, Taylor polynomials, sequences and series, power series. Credit will not be given for both MATH 1248 and MATH 1242. No credit following receipt of credit for MATH 2248 or above. Prerequisite: C- or better in MATH 1234.
nodes.other_requirements: C- or better in MATH 1234. C- or better in MATH 021.

nodes.title: Climate Change I
nodes.description: Explores how and when climate has changed over time and its impact on people and ecosystems; how humans have altered Earth's climate historically; how climate will change in the future; and implications for people and planet. Learn to communicate about climate change and take action. Credit not awarded for both ENSC 1490 and ENSC 2490.

nodes.title: Intro Environmental Sci
nodes.description: Explores the complex interactions between humans and environmental systems and the ecological foundations and scientific principles to better understand how the coupled human-natural system works, and how science can be used to help solve environmental problems.

nodes.title: Intro to Geog Info Systems
nodes.description: Understanding and application of computer-based, geographically-referenced information systems. Prerequisite: Minimum Sophomore standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Minimum Sophomore standing. Must register for a NR 2430 lab A01 - A06

nodes.title: Global Environmental Assessmnt
nodes.description: Introduction to skills for assessing human impacts on the global environment. Theory and application of GPS, geographic information systems and satellite remote sensing to address key environmental issues. Prerequisites: ENSC 1010, ENVS 1500, GEOG 1200, NR 1010, NR 1090, or Geospatial Technologies minor.
nodes.other_requirements: Geospatial Technologies minor

nodes.title: Recovery&Restor Altered Ecosys
nodes.description: Role of stress and disturbance and the natural process of recovery in aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems. Human efforts to modify, restore, and remediate altered ecosystems. Prerequisites: NR 2030, BCOR 1450, or BCOR 2100; minimum Junior standing.
nodes.other_requirements: minimum Junior standing; Some labs meeting take place off-campus; Must register for a lab section concurrently;Juniors and Seniors Only.

nodes.title: Ecology, Ecosystems & Environ
nodes.description: Major ecological concepts and their application. Analysis of form, structure, and function of organisms, populations, communities, ecosystems, and landscapes. Prerequisites: RSENR students or Agroecology or Landscape Design or Environmental Studies majors; minimum Sophomore standing.
nodes.other_requirements: minimum Sophomore standing

nodes.title: Fundamentals of Calculus II
nodes.description: Techniques and applications of integration. An introduction to multi-variable calculus: partial derivatives and double integrals. Prerequisite: MATH 1212 or MATH 1234.
nodes.other_requirements: Open to Degree and PACE students

nodes.title: Internship
nodes.description: On-site supervised work experience combined with a structured academic learning plan directed by a faculty member or a faculty-staff team in which a faculty member is the instructor of record, for which academic credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission required

nodes.title: Undergraduate Research
nodes.description: Undergraduate student work on individual or small team research projects under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion. Up to six hours. Three can be applied to elected concentration with Director permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission required; completion of an Experiential Learning plan required

nodes.title: VT: Natural & Cultural Hst
nodes.description: Introduction to the Vermont landscape that combines elements of natural history, field ecology, and environmental history. Students visit locations around the Champlain Valley as they build observational skills, study natural systems, and examine past and present human relationships with nature.

nodes.title: Calculus I
nodes.description: Introduction to calculus of functions of one variable including: limits, continuity, techniques and applications of differentiation and integration. Credit not given for more than one course in the pair MATH 1212, MATH 1234 unless followed by MATH 1248 or MATH 1242. No credit following receipt of credit for MATH 1248 or above. Prerequisite: C- or better in MATH 1034 or a score of 76 or higher on the placement assessment.
nodes.other_requirements: C- or better in MATH 1034 or a score of 76 or higher on the placement assessment. Prereqs enforced by the system.

nodes.title: Applied Envir Assess Analysis
nodes.description: Approaches used to identify, evaluate, and manage environmental risks. Focus on interactions among ecological, economic, and social considerations; often utilizing a watershed perspective. Problem formulation, methods selection. Case studies. Project-oriented. Prerequisites: ENSC 2030 or NR 2430; NR 3050 or SEP 2880; minimum Junior standing.
nodes.other_requirements: minimum Junior standing. Must register for one discussion section ENSC 4020

nodes.title: Exploring Biology 2
nodes.description: An evolutionary perspective to exploring biology. Topics include: patterns of inheritance, Darwinian evolution, evolution of biodiversity, ecology of organisms, human effects on biological systems. Credit not awarded for both BCOR 1450 and BIOL 1450.

nodes.title: Fundamentals of Calculus I
nodes.description: Introduction to limits and differential/integral calculus with a wide variety of applications. Students interested in intensive use of mathematics should take MATH 1234. Credit not awarded for both MATH 1212 and MATH 1234 unless followed by MATH 1248. See MATH 1242. Prerequisite: C- or better in MATH 1012 or C- or better in MATH 1034, or a score of 61 or higher on the placement assessment.
nodes.other_requirements: C- or better in MATH 1012 or C- or better in MATH 1034, or a score of 61 or higher on the placement assessment. Prereqs enforced by the system: MATH 1012 or MATH 1034 or ALEKS MPA of at least 61; Open to Degree and PACE students

nodes.title: Pollutant Mvmt/Air,Land&Water
nodes.description: Physical, chemical, and biological aspects of pollutant behavior from source to ultimate fate. Laboratory methodologies for measuring pollutants and predicting their transport, behavior, and fate. Prerequisites: CHEM 1400, CHEM 1450, MATH 1212 or MATH 1234; MATH 1224 or MATH 1248.
nodes.other_requirements: Must register for an ENSC 3600 Lab; ENSC Majors only; Minimum Junior standing; Open to Degree and PACE students.

nodes.title: Environmental Science Honors
nodes.description: College honors thesis or other department/program honors, under the supervision of a faculty member. Offered at department discretion. Prerequisites: Senior standing; Instructor permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Senior standing; Instructor permission. Instructor permission required

nodes.title: Sustainability Science
nodes.description: The study of sustainability integrating natural and social science perspectives. Topics include theories of ecological adaptation and resilience, sustainability assessment methods, life cycle analysis, relational values, community science, emerging technologies and their applications to achieving a sustainability transformation. Prerequisites: ENVS 1500, ENVS 1510, NR 1010, NR 1020, NR 1090, or Instructor permission; minimum Sophomore standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission; minimum Sophomore standing

nodes.title: CL:Ecosys Mgt:Intg Sci,Soc&Pol
nodes.description: Analysis of the interaction between science and politics in ecosystem management. Consideration of various types of science and their roles in shaping environmental management, politics, and policy. Interdisciplinary application of course concepts to case studies of complex ecological problems. Prerequisites: NR 2030, NR 2040.
nodes.other_requirements: RSENR students only; Mimimum Junior standing

nodes.title: Weather, Climate & Landscapes
nodes.description: Introduction to the fundamentals of weather, climate, landform evolution, and plant distribution using a systems approach. Focus on variation in processes over space and time.
nodes.other_requirements: First year and sophomores only during week of registration; then Juniors & seniors can enroll, space permitting; Junior and senior GEOG majors contact instructor; requires internet access and ability to log onto Brightspace and MSTeams; requires a working camera on computer or device if using MSTeams.

nodes.title: Undergraduate Research
nodes.description: Undergraduate student work on individual or small team research projects under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Application and Instructor permission required

nodes.title: Independent Study
nodes.description: A course which is tailored to fit the interests of a specific student, which occurs outside the traditional classroom/laboratory setting under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission required. Offered at department discretion.
